title: Tlingit-oriented classes give students a boost
summary: JUNEAU -- Tlingit-oriented classrooms at a Juneau elementary school are being hailed as a success. Students enrolled in the Harborview Elementary School program generally perform as well as other students in the district and do better than Native students on average, a recent study shows. "This whole emphasis on literacy is paying off," Annie Calkins, a former district administrator who has studied the program, told the Juneau School Board last week. Eunice James-Lee's son Hunter, 9, has been enrolled in the Tlingit program for three years. "For the chance for our kids to succeed in school, to see them thrive, to see them develop, to grow in confidence -- I wanted that for my children -- and to know who they are, where they're from," she said. The Tlingit classrooms have operated for three…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Hunters use variety of techniques to track down big game By KEN MARSH Daily News correspondent (Published: July 6, 2003) Big-game hunting in Alaska is a science involving planning, strategy and refined backcountry skills. Ultimately, however, the heart of the hunt, the part that defines its success or failure, is a hunter's ability to find and get within range of the game. Three hunting options most popular in Alaska are spot-and-stalk, still hunting and calling. Each has its own advantages under the right conditions, depending upon the game you seek. • Spot-and-stalk hunting is particularly effective in mountainous country or sparsely treed taiga and tundra areas where game can be seen from long distances. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Man says Agent Orange buried in Tok SIX BARRELS: Former employee of contractor says he helped bury it 30 years ago. The Associated Press (Published: July 28, 2003) FAIRBANKS -- The former employee of a contractor hired by the Army told the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner that he helped bury Agent Orange in Tok 30 years ago. John Erickson said he wondered at the time if he was doing the right thing given the health concerns raised by the use of Agent Orange as a defoliant during the Vietnam War. But Erickson said the Army command at Fort Greely gave orders to the contractor to dig a big hole and bury the six, 55-gallon barrels. summary: | | | Pilot's husband sues fuel company FATAL CRASH: Wrongful death suit filed against Evert's Air Fuel over accident that killed four. The Associated Press (Published: July 28, 2003) FAIRBANKS -- A lawsuit has been filed claiming that a fuel company is to blame for a July 2001 crash that killed a pilot and three passengers. Fairbanks doctor Nancy Lewis, 48, and a couple from Albuquerque, N.M., and their son, were killed in the crash. Lewis' husband, Hugh Rose, filed a wrongful death lawsuit against Evert's Air Fuel exactly two years after the death of his wife and the others. summary: | | | 1 dies, 2 survive after Cook Inlet skiff flips SELDOVIA: Trio were en route to Nanwalek; Coast Guard rescues others. By WESLEY LOY Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 4, 2003) One boater died but two others survived after their skiff overturned during a Cook Inlet journey Saturday night from Seldovia to the village of Nanwalek, the U.S. Coast Guard reported. The 14-foot skiff left Seldovia at 7:35 p.m. and was due to make Nanwalek in about an hour. It was reported overdue three hours after departure, and the Coast Guard launched an HH-60 Jayhawk helicopter from Kodiak to search for the craft. summary: | | | Beluga count low so far COOK INLET: Biologists aren't alarmed; special cameras reveal observers aren't tallying them all. Inlet's mammals counted By DOUG O'HARRA Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 4, 2003) A team of biologists searching for beluga whales in Cook Inlet recorded the lowest count ever during the annual summer airborne survey, but they say it doesn't necessarily mean the population has gone down. The 174 whales seen by people flying at 110 mph about 800 feet above the roiling surface in June were only the beginning of a five-month calculation needed to produce the 2003 population estimate. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | ACS rings up bigger profit SPINOFF: Yellow pages business now operates as a separate company. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 2, 2003) Alaska Communications Systems reported a $92.7 million second-quarter profit, its bottom line bolstered by the spinoff of the Anchorage-based telecom company's yellow pages business, which netted it about $98 million. Second-quarter revenue fell 8.7 percent, totaling $84.8 million. That includes revenue from the yellow pages business, which was spun off in May and now operates as a separate company called ACS Media. During last year's second quarter, profit was $3.6 million and revenue totaled $92.9 million. summary: | | | Today's low interest rates have a dark side CHRIS STEPHENS REAL ESTATE (Published: August 3, 2003) While the current low interest rates are a boon to buyers and sellers of real estate, these low rates can have a dark side: they can make properties unsalable. Low interest rates are a tremendous benefit. The lower the interest rate, the lower the debt service, which gives buyers more purchasing power. Lower interest rates also lower capitalization rates, increasing the value of properties. The dark side of low interest rates occurs for properties with existing debt that has either a higher interest rate than can be obtained at the current time and that cannot be paid off and replaced with a new lower-rate loan, or loans that have what is called a "yield-maintenance provision." summary: | | | Divers save Alaska-born eagle CALIFORNIA: Bird set free on island found floundering offshore. The Associated Press (Published: October 17, 2002) CHANNEL ISLANDS NATIONAL PARK, Calif. -- An Alaska-born bald eagle trying desperately to stay afloat amid the waves of the Pacific Ocean was rescued by two divers who saw the bird about to go under. The eagle, set free last month as part of an effort to reintroduce the species to the Channel Islands, was recovering this week at the Ojai Raptor Center, said Patty Perry, who works at the nonprofit facility. The 4-month-old female was malnourished and dehydrated when Mark Ferguson spotted it floundering eight miles off Santa Cruz Island last week.
